vignette: A 64-year-old male presents for his routine 6-month surveillance ultrasound due to chronic hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection with compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A). He has a history of successful antiviral treatment for HCV, achieving sustained virological response (SVR) 3 years ago. His ECOG performance status is 0. Recent laboratory tests show normal liver function, with an albumin of 3.9 g/dL, total bilirubin of 0.8 mg/dL, and INR of 1.1. Platelet count is 110,000/µL. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) has historically been stable at 8 ng/mL, but the current level is 45 ng/mL. The ultrasound reveals a new, hypoechoic nodule measuring 2.7 cm in the right lobe, which was not present on the previous scan. No other abnormalities are noted. He denies any recent weight loss, abdominal pain, or other constitutional symptoms.
hypothesis: confirming the diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) non-invasively
new_information: A subsequent contrast-enhanced MRI shows the 2.7 cm nodule with arterial phase hyperenhancement and portal venous washout, categorized as LI-RADS 5.

vignette: A 62-year-old male presents for routine follow-up. He denies new symptoms like weight loss, abdominal pain, or jaundice. His performance status is ECOG 0. He is very anxious about this new finding and concerned about his treatment options.

new_information: The pathology report confirms a solitary, well-differentiated HCC with clear margins and no microvascular invasion.

He now reports mild, intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort, but denies weight loss, fever, or jaundice. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show AFP of 350 ng/mL (previous was 15 ng/mL). A contrast-enhanced MRI of the liver is performed, revealing a new 2.8 cm arterial phase hyperenhancing lesion with clear portal venous washout in segment VII. No other suspicious lesions or vascular invasion are noted. The patient has a good ECOG PS of 0 and no clinically significant portal hypertension on previous assessment. He is keen to understand his treatment options.
hypothesis: Implementing regular CT or MRI surveillance every 3-4 months for the next two years.
new_information: The patient successfully underwent curative radiofrequency ablation for the single HCC lesion.

vignette: A 65-year-old male with a history of chronic Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) infection, successfully treated with sustained virological response (SVR) five years ago, presents for routine hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) surveillance. He has known compensated cirrhosis, currently Child-Pugh A, and an ECOG performance status of 0. His medical history also includes well-controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes. He reports no new symptoms such as abdominal pain, weight loss, or fatigue. His last surveillance ultrasound six months ago was unremarkable. During the current ultrasound examination, a new, solitary hepatic nodule measuring 2.5 cm in diameter is identified in segment VII. Subsequent triphasic contrast-enhanced CT confirms a 2.5 cm lesion demonstrating typical arterial hyperenhancement and portal venous phase washout, highly suspicious for HCC. Laboratory results show an AFP level of 120 ng/mL (normal <20 ng/mL), normal liver function tests, and a platelet count of 150,000/µL.
hypothesis: If you were thinking of recommending surgical resection as the primary treatment for this patient's HCC.
new_information: and then you find that the tumor is located centrally, making complete surgical resection technically challenging with an inadequate future liver remnant.

vignette: A 62-year-old male presents for a routine follow-up.
